Facts.text: Battle of Saucourt: French King Louis III defeats the Vikings
Louis VI "The Fat," King of France, is crowned at the cathedral in Orléans after his half-brother prevents him from reaching Reims
Tragedy of Mal Saint-Martin: 200 practitioners from Liège lose their lives in the Church of Saint-Martin, set on fire by the "Petits" (tradespeople class)
First known letter is sent from North America by John Rut from St. John's in Newfoundland
Peace of the Ladies (Treaty of Cambrai) is made between the Holy Roman Empire and France, negotiated by Louise of Savoy and Margaret of Austria
Dutch States General meet at Valenciennes
German astronomer and priest David Fabricius discovers the light variation of Mira, the first variable star

Tokugawa Iemitsu, the third Tokugawa shogun, establishes alternate attendance (sankin kotai) where feudal daimyōs spend 1 year at Edo Castle (Tokyo) and 1 year at their feudal manor, while their families remain in Tokyo as hostages (Traditional Japanese Date: June 21)
2,000 men from the VOC army surround the city of Malacca
Zorilla's "Bire el Ojo" premieres in Toledo
Battle of Allersheim: French defeat Bavarians
Dutch Stadtholder William II and Amsterdam reach an accord about a standing army
Nathaniel Bacon publishes "Declaration of People of Virginia"
Robert LaSalle builds the first ship in America, a brig named the Griffon
English and Dutch fleet under George Rooke and George Byng capture Gibraltar
Battle of Trencsén: Austria defeats Hungarian rebellion army
Teatro alla Scala opens in Milan
Emperor Francis I permits Jews who serve in the military in the "Countries of the Bohemian Crown" to marry non-Jews
HMS Beagle reaches the river mouth of the Rio Negro
America's first intercollegiate sporting event takes place when the Harvard heavyweight rowing crew beats Yale by 2 lengths over 2 miles on Lake Winnipesaukee, New Hampshire
Rotterdam-Gouda railway opens
The Second Māori War begins in New Zealand
Federal fleet bombards Galveston, Texas
Governor Seymour asks President Lincoln to suspend draft in New York
The American Canoe Association is founded at Lake George, NY, with the first Commodore being William L. Alden
Boers sign Convention of Pretoria: Transvaal becomes semi-autonomous
US National Lawn Tennis Association removes "National" from its name
US Congress passes the first law restricting immigration
John McNally's musical "Good Mr Best" premieres in NYC
British journalist Francis Younghusband visits the forbidden city of Lhasa
Washington Nationals' Tom Hughes becomes the first MLB pitcher to win a 1-0 extra innings game with his own home run in the 10th inning against the St. Louis Browns

Emperor Wilhelm of Germany meets with Tsar Nicholas of Russia to discuss Germany's plan to build a railroad to Baghdad; the discussion helps move Russia towards Britain and eventually the Triple Entente
French brothers Amédée and Jean Bouyssonie discover the fossil remains of a nearly complete 60,000-year-old Neanderthal man, known as the "Old Man of La Chapelle," at La Chapelle-aux-Saints, France
Wheatland Hop Riot on a California farm kills four peole in one of the first farm labor disputes
Belgium rejects demand to allow free crossing for German Army leading to their invasion hours later
First unofficial trip through the Panama Canal by the SS Christobel
French fleet sails to North Africa
German battle cruiser Goeben leaves Messina
Germany invades Belgium and declares war on France, beginning World War I
World Alliance for Promoting International Friendship through churches forms
Yankees catcher Les Nunamaker throws out three Tigers' runners at second base during the first inning, the only time in the 20th century
The first Allied troops land at Archangel, the Russian port on the White Sea
First aerial crop dusting in Troy, Ohio, to kill caterpillars
MLB Commissioner Kenesaw Landis hands out lifetime bans to eight Chicago White Sox players accused in the Black Sox scandal despite their acquittal by a Chicago jury
Last US troops leave Nicaragua (there since 1912)
Traffic lights installed at Piccadilly Circus, London
Ray Barbuti wins the 400 m in 47.8 at the Amsterdam Olympics, the only individual track event won by an American at the Games

Official automatic timing and photo-finish camera for track events are used for the first time at the Los Angeles Olympics, instrumental in changing the 110 m hurdles final; review gives Donald Finlay bronze ahead of American Jack Keller
Philadelphia A's defeat NY Yankees 7-0, the first time in 308 games "Bronx Bombers" are shut out
Karl Hein (Olympic record of 56.49) and Erwin Blask achieve a German 1-2 in the hammer throw at the Berlin Olympics
German occupiers in France impose censorship rules on films, requiring approval prior to exhibition and forbidding the depiction of German losses in WWI
Italian troops invade British Somalia
Lithuanian SSR is accepted into USSR
Seaplane Clare makes first British passenger flight to the US
Gas (petroleum) sales limited in US
German troops conquer Roslavl in Russia
Nazi occupiers attack the city of Orel and leave it in flames
Allied troops under Lieutenant General Joseph Stilwell take Myitkyina, Burma
General Montgomery visits General Dempsey's headquarters
Belgian government of Huysmans forms
American divers Vicki Draves, Zoe Ann Olsen and Patsy Elsener go 1-2-3 in a US clean sweep of the medals in the women's 3 m springboard at the London Olympics
An American 1-2 in the men's 200 m at the London Olympics with Mel Patton (21.1) defeating teammate Barney Ewell
An American clean sweep of the medals in the men's 3 m springboard diving at the London Olympics with Bruce Harlan winning gold, Miller Anderson silver and Sammy Lee bronze
An American sweep of the medals in the men's shot put at the London Olympics with Wilbur Thompson taking gold ahead of teammates Jim Delaney and Jim Fuchs
Franklin D. Roosevelt advisor Alger Hiss is accused of being a "communist"
The US 4 x 200 m freestyle relay team of Wally Ris, Jimmy McLane, Wally Wolf, and Bill Smith swim a world record of 8:46.0 to win gold at the London Olympics
Basketball Association of America (BAA) and National Basketball League (NBL) merge to form the National Basketball Association (NBA), with Maurice Podoloff elected as head of the new league
Republic of Indonesia proclaims ceasefire
William H Jackson ends term as deputy director of CIA
XV Summer Olympic Games close at the Helsingin Olympiastadion, Helsinki, Finland
Frank Blair becomes news anchor of "Today Show"
The Convair XFY-1 Pogo, a VTOL (vertical takeoff and landing) aircraft, makes its first successful flight
Belgian middle-distance runner Roger Moens breaks Rudolf Harbig's long-standing 800 m world record (1:45.7) in Oslo, Norway
Hurricane Connie begins pounding US for 11 days, making landfall in North Carolina and traveling to the Great Lakes
American sprinter Willie Williams sets a 100 m world record at 10.1 in Berlin, Germany
British offensive against Imam Ghalib bin Ali of Oman
MLB Baltimore Orioles manager Paul Richards is ejected from both games of a doubleheader at Tiger Stadium in Detroit, Michigan
USS Nautilus reaches the North Pole, becoming the first submarine to achieve a submarine transit of the North Pole
AL defeats NL 5-3 in Game 2 of the All-Star Baseball doubleheader to boost the player pension fund at LA Memorial Coliseum
Fifty killed in uprising in Guinea-Bissau
For the only time in MLB history, teams exchange managers as Detroit trades Jimmy Dykes (44-52) for Cleveland's Joe Gordon (49-46)
Lee Petty and his sons Richard and Maurice race against each other for the first and only time at Dixie Speedway in Birmingham, Alabama; Richard finishes second, Lee third
Niger gains independence from France
Pittsburgh Steelers (NFL) defeat Toronto Argonauts (CFL) 43-16 in an exhibition game at CNE Stadium in Toronto
Pittsburgh Pirates rout St. Louis Cardinals 19-0, the largest lopsided shutout in NL history
29th Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: Green Bay 42, All-Stars 20 (65,000 attendees)
NY Met Frank Thomas hits his sixth home run in three games
Beatles' final performance at the Cavern Club in Liverpool, England

Warner Bros. Records releases the single of Allan Sherman's novelty song "Hello Muddah, Hello Fadduh (A Letter from Camp)," set to the music of Amilcare Ponchielli's "Dance of the Hours" from his 1876 opera "La Gioconda"
South African government bans records by The Beatles
45,000 US soldiers are sent to Vietnam
James Law rides the entire New York City subway in 22 hours and 12 minutes
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Cincinnati Reds, with 25 hits and 10 runs in the 5th, edge Philadelphia Phillies 19-17 at Connie Mack Stadium
Christopher Hampton's "Philanthropist" premieres in London
Hurricane "Celia" becomes the most expensive Gulf storm in history
Mairiam Hargrave of Yorkshire passes her driving test on her 40th try
The second NFL work stoppage ends with NFL and Players' Association agreeing to a 4-year, $19.1 million deal
Chozen-ji/International Zen Dojo is founded by Omori Sogen Roshi in Hawaii
US Senate ratifies the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty with the Soviet Union to limit the use of missile systems capable of defending against missile-delivered nuclear weapons
Flash fire kills 51 at an amusement park on the Isle of Man, UK
National People's party wins Dutch Antilles National elections
Chartered Boeing 707 crashes in Atlas Mountains near Agadir, Morocco while descending in heavy fog for scheduled landing at Inezgane Airport, killing 188
Louisiana Superdome football stadium is dedicated in New Orleans, Louisiana
Poland and West Germany reach accord about returning ethnic Germans
Two riverboats collide and sink in China's West River, killing 500 people
Radio Shack introduces the TRS-80 personal computer, and within weeks, thousands are ordered
United States Senate hearing on MKULTRA
Eleventh Commonwealth Games open in Edmonton, Canada
Fastest jai-alai shot at 188 mph (302.5 km/h) by José Arieto at Newport Jai Alai, Rhode Island
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Al Kaline, Duke Snider,Chuck Klein, and Tom Yawkey are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame
XXII Summer Olympic Games close at the Grand Arena of the Central Lenin Stadium in Moscow, Russia
France performs a nuclear test in the Pacific
Senegalese opposition parties, under the leadership of Mamadou Dia, launch the Anti-imperialist Action Front-Suxxali Reew Mi
Pitching coach Clyde King becomes the New York Yankees' third manager of the season, replacingGene Michaelafter the Yankees are swept at home by the Chicago White Sox
John Sain of South Bend, Indiana, builds a 3.91-meter (12.83-foot) house of cards
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site

17-year-old Australian swimmer Jon Sieben stages a withering finish to upset hot favorite Michael Gross of West Germany in the 200 m butterfly at the Los Angeles Olympics in a world record time of 1:57.04
Bomb attack on Madras India airport kills 32 people
Mary Lou Retton scores 10 for final vault to win the individual all-round competition in Los Angeles and become the first American woman to win an Olympic gymnastics medal
Mexicans Ernesto Canto and Raúl González take the gold and silver medals in the 20 km walk at the Los Angeles Olympics
"Nihilator" sets harness pacing mile (1:49.6) in East Rutherford, NJ
Train crash in Flaujac, France, kills 35 people
First NFL "American Bowl" exhibition game at London's Wembley Stadium; Chicago Bears defeat Dallas Cowboys 17-6
Discovery in the Orbital Processing Facility is powered up for STS-26
Twins pitcher Joe Niekro is caught with a file on the mound in an 11-3 win against the Angels, ejected and suspended for 10 games
David "Skip" Storch (37) swims 152.9 miles (246 km) of the Hudson River from Albany to NYC
Cincinnati Reds set an MLB record for the most hits in the first inning of a game with 16, scoring 14 runs in an 18-1 rout of the Houston Astros
Jorge Velásquez wins aboard Maddie Bumpo in the 3rd at Arlington International Racecourse to become only the fifth jockey in the world to ride 6,000 career winners
Lawrence Delisle drives his four kids into a river
98.8°F (37.1°C) in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire (UK record)
New York Yankees rookie first baseman Kevin Maas sets an MLB record by hitting 10 home runs in the fewest at-bats (72)
Radio Kuwait goes off air due to the Iraqi invasion
US announces commitment of naval forces to Gulf region
Giant Victory, driven by Jack Moiseyev, wins the Hambletonian, securing a second $1 million purse in 22 days for Moiseyev after winning the Meadowlands Pace
Paramount Studios inaugurates New York Street on TV/movie lot
102.4°F (39.1°C) in Tokyo, Japan (record)
First Jordanian plane to fly over Israeli airspace (King Hussein pilot)
CNN en Español premieres

"Macarena" by Los Del Rio starts a 14-week run at No. 1 on the US singles chart; sales of 11 million copies worldwide lead to its ranking as the No. 1 Greatest One-Hit Wonder of All Time by VH1 in 2002
US General William F. Garrison accepts responsibility for outcome of the 1993 raid in Somalia, and retires from military service
Manchester United defeats Chelsea 4-2 on penalties to win the Charity Shield after a bad-tempered 1-1 draw at Wembley
Oued El-Had and Mezouara massacre in Algeria kills 40-76 villagers
Sky Tower opens in Auckland, New Zealand, standing at 328 meters (1,076 feet) as the tallest freestanding structure in the Southern Hemisphere
The Oval hosts its first competitive cricket match played under floodlights in London as the home team Surrey loses to Sussex by 8 wickets in the day/night 40-over fixture
England cricket wicket-keeper Alec Stewart becomes only the 4th batsman to score a century (105) in his 100th Test, during the 3rd Test against the West Indies at Old Trafford

South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Amnesty Committee grants amnesty to Curnick Ndlovu, the KwaZulu-Natal leader of the African National Congress and United Democratic Front, for committing sabotage during December 1961 and June 1963 near Durban

Olympic champion Gezahegne Abera of Ethiopia becomes the first athlete to achieve the Olympics-World Championships marathon double, running 2:12:42 to defeat Kenya's Simon Biwott by just 0.1 seconds in the Edmonton World Championships
The Real IRA detonate a car bomb in Ealing, London, injuring 7 people
MESSENGER spacecraft launches from Cape Canaveral
President Maaouya Ould Sid'Ahmed Taya of Mauritania is overthrown in a military coup while attending the funeral of King Fahd in Saudi Arabia
Brazilian Champ Car driver Cristiano da Matta crashes into a deer during testing at Road America, Wisconsin; seriously injured but returns to racing in 2008
American Missy Franklin swims a world record 2:04.06 to win the 200 m backstroke gold medal at the London Olympics
United Nations General Assembly reproaches The United Nations Security Council over its lack of action in Syria
80 people are killed in a wave of insurgency across Iraq
Roman Empire generalTiberiusdefeats Dalmatians on the river Bathinus
Deposed Patriarch of Constantinople Nestorius, considered the originator of Nestorianism, exiled by Byzantine EmperorTheodosius IIto a monastery in Egypt
Battle of Steenkerque: French defeatWilliam III's combined English/Dutch army

Battle of the Nile: British AdmiralHoratio Nelsonforces the remnants of the French fleet to surrender, concluding a decisive victory for the British, who capture or destroy 11 French ships of the line and 2 frigates
NaturalistAlexander von Humboldtlands in Bordeaux, France, completing his five-year expedition to Latin America
Gioachino Rossini's last and greatest opera "Guillaume Tell" (William Tell) premieres at Salle Le Peletier in Paris
FirestoneTire and Rubber Company founded in Akron, Ohio, by Harvey Firestone
MLB umpire Tim Hurst instigates a riot by spitting in the face of A's second basemanEddie Collins, who questioned a call; Hurst is banned for life
British Foreign SecretaryEdward Greyfamously remarks, "The lamps are going out all over Europe. We shall not see them lit again in our life-time."
Baseball games are canceled following the death of US PresidentWarren G. Harding
Vice PresidentCalvin Coolidgebecomes the 30th US President upon the death of President Warren G. Harding
Dutch track cyclistPiet Moeskopswins his fourth consecutive UCI World Sprint Championship in Paris
For the second time in the year, Phillies' outfielderChuck Kleinhits safely in 26 straight games, but the run is halted in the second game of a doubleheader against the Boston Braves
Adolf Hitlermerges the offices of German Chancellor and President, declaring himself "Führer" (leader)
Jean Genet's play "Ondine" premieres in Paris
Joe DiMaggiogoes 0-4 in the Yankees' 6-2 loss to the St. Louis Browns, ending his on-base streak of 74 games, second in MLB history
GeneralGeorge S. Pattonslaps a US GI in a hospital, accusing him of cowardice
Auschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp gasses 4,000 Romani people
Negro League veteran pitcherSatchel Paige, age 42, makes his first start for Cleveland Indians, and goes 7 innings in a 5-3 win against Washington
Italian Ferrari driverAlberto Ascariclinches the Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship by winning the German Grand Prix at the Nürburgring

100,000 attend two-day Newport Pop Festival in Costa Mesa, California; performers includedAlice Cooper, Canned Heat, The Chambers Brothers, Country Joe and the Fish, Paul Butterfield Blues Band,Sonny & Cher, Steppenwolf,Tiny Tim, The Animals, Grateful Dead, Jefferson Airplane, Quicksilver Messenger Service, and The Byrds
Paul McCartneyannounces the formation of the group "Wings," consisting of himself, his wifeLinda McCartney, and former Moody Blues guitarist Denny Laine
British Prime MinisterEdward Heathproclaims an emergency crisis due to a dock strike
Tamla/Motown Records releases "Innervisions," the 16th studio album byStevie Wonder, including the hits "Higher Ground" and "Don't You Worry 'Bout a Thing"
Jeff Baxter, guitarist and founding member ofSteely Dan, quits the band and joins the Doobie Brothers

13,000 air traffic controllers (PATCO) begin their strike; US PresidentRonald Reaganstates during a press conference, "If they do not report for work within 48 hours they have forfeited their jobs and will be terminated"
Willie McCovey,Bobby Doerr, and Ernie Lombardi are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, NY
Detroit Tigers aceJack Morristies the AL record with five wild pitches in a 4-2, 10-inning loss against the Kansas City Royals
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonsets an AL record of 50 stolen bases in 9 of his first 11 big-league seasons
"Unforgiven," directed byClint Eastwoodand starring Clint Eastwood,Gene Hackman, andMorgan Freeman, premieres in Los Angeles (Academy Awards Best Picture 1993)
AmericanAndre Agassiwins the men's singles tennis gold medal at the Atlanta Olympics, defeating SpaniardSergi Bruguera6-2, 6-3, 6-1
Irish bandBoyzonerelease their best-selling single "No Matter What," composed byAndrew Lloyd WebberandJim Steinman
American country music singerPatsy Clineis posthumously awarded a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame
Arsenal FC completes a huge signing coup, snapping up ace French strikerThierry Henryfor an estimated fee of £11 million from Italian club Juventus
"The Princess Diaries," starringAnne Hathawayin her film debut, is released nationwide
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushsigns into law the Iran and Libya Sanctions Act (ILSA) Extension Act of 2001
Bob Dylanreturns to the Newport Folk Festival for the first time since his controversial electric set in 1965
St. Louis first basemanAlbert Pujolsbecomes the first player in MLB history to hit at least 30 home runs in each of his first four seasons as the Cardinals beat Montreal 10-6
Barry Bonds' 715th MLB home run ball, which moves him past Babe Ruth for second all-time, fetches $220,100 at auction
Morgan Freemanis injured in an automobile accident near Ruleville, Mississippi, when his car flips over several times on the highway
American super-swimmerMichael Phelpswins the 100 m butterfly in London to take his all-time Olympic gold medal tally to a record 17
Christopher Columbus, with Juan de la Cosa as second-in-command, sets sail on his first voyage with three ships: Santa María, Pinta, and Niña from Palos de la Frontera, Spain, for the "Indies"
American sprinterJesse Owenswins the 100 m in 10.3 seconds in front of Adolf Hitler in a famous race at the Berlin Olympics, the first of four gold medals he wins at the Games
Thepedestal of the Statue of Libertyreopens after being closed since the September 11, 2001 attacks
Étienne Dolet, French scholar and printer, born in Orléans, France (d. 1546)
August Kuhnel, German composer and musician, born in Delmenhorst, County of Oldenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. ca 1700)
John Henley, English clergyman, born in Melton Mowbray, Leicestershire, England (d. 1759)
George Hervey, 2nd Earl of Bristol, English statesman and ambassador to Madrid, born in England (d. 1775)
Richard Caswell, American lawyer, Revolutionary War soldier, and politician (1st Governor of North Carolina, 1776-80 & 1785-87), born in Harford County, Maryland (d. 1789)
Carl Ludwig Junker, German composer, born in Kirchberg an der Jagst, Schwäbisch Hall, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1797)
Charles Stanhope, 3rd earl Stanhope, English radical politician and scientist, born in London (d. 1816)
Frederik William III, King of Prussia (1797-1840), born in Potsdam, Prussia (d. 1840)
Joseph Paxton, English landscape architect (Crystal Palace), born in Milton Bryan, Bedfordshire (d. 1865)

Wilson Cary Swann, American physician, philanthropist, social reformer, and namesake of landmark fountain in Philadelphia's Logan Square, born in Alexandria, District of Columbia (now Virginia) (d. 1876)
Hamilton Fish, American politician (Governor of New York), born in New York City (d. 1893)
John E. Smith, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Berne, Switzerland (d. 1897)
Archduke Albrecht of Austria, Duke of Teschen, Austrian general (Battle at Custozza), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1895)
Joseph Schadde, Flemish architect (New Stock exchange Antwerp), born in Antwerp (d. 1894)
Luther Orlando Emerson, American composer, born in Parsonsfield, Maine (d. 1915)
William Miller, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Ithaca, New York (d. 1909)
Francisco Barbieri, Spanish zarzuela opera composer (El Diablo en El Poder), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1894)
Thomas Francis Meagre, Irish nationalist and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Waterford, Ireland (d. 1867)
William Burnham Woods, American politician, judge, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Newark, Ohio (d. 1887)
Ivan Zajc, Croatian composer and conductor, born in Fiume, Kingdom of Hungary, Austrian Empire (d. 1914)
Auguste Schmidt, German teacher and feminist, born in Breslau, Germany (d. 1902)
Yamagata Aritomo, Japanese soldier and statesman (Prime Minister of Japan 1889–91,1898–1900), born in Hagi, Japan (d. 1922)
Jan de Louter, Dutch lawyer and tutor of Dutch Queen Wilhelmina, born in Amersfoort, Netherlands (d. 1932)
Paulus Adrianus Daum, Dutch writer, founder and editor (Batavian Newspaper), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1898)
Reginald Heber Roe, 1st Vice-Chancellor of the University of Queensland and 2nd Headmaster of Brisbane Grammar School, born in Blandford, Dorset, England (d. 1926)
Isabella Caroline Somerset, British philanthropist, temperance leader and campaigner for women's rights, born in London (d. 1921)
Fernand de La Tombelle, French organist and composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1928)
Joe Hunter, English cricket wicket-keeper (5 Tests, 11 dismissals; Yorkshire CCC), born in Scarborough, Yorkshire, England (d. 1891)
William Kennedy Dickson, Scottish inventor (devised an early motion picture camera), born in Le Minihic-sur-Rance, Brittany, France (d. 1935)
Vernon Louis Parrington, American author (Romantic Revolution, Pulitzer 1928), born in Aurora, Illinois (d. 1929)
Haakon VII, King of Norway (1905-57), born in Charlottenlund Palace, Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1957)
Albert Marie Léon Adolphe de Bassompierre, Belgian ambassador to Tokyo (1921-39), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1956)[1]
Eugène Baie, Belgian author (Sub rosa et sub umbra), born in Anderlecht, Belgium (d. 1963)
Constantino Gaito, Argentinian composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1945)
Segundo Luis Moreno Andrade, Ecuadorian composer and musicologist, born in Cotacachi, Ecuador (d. 1972)
Louis Gruenberg, Russian-American composer (Daniel Jazz), born in Brest-Litovsk, Russia (d. 1964)
Siegfried Salomon, Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1962)
Leslie Henson, English comedian and actor (Lady Luck, Funny Face, Harvey), born in Notting Hill, London (d. 1957)
Oscar van Hemel, Belgian-Dutch violinist and composer, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1981)
Harry Heilmann, American Baseball HOF outfielder (4 × AL batting champion, Detroit Tigers) and broadcaster (WXYZ), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1951)
Johan van Maarseveen, Dutch Minister of Justice and Internal minister, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1951)
Marguerite Nichols, American actress (An Old Man's Folly), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1941)
Karl Kehrle, Benedictine monk and beekeeper, born in Mittelbiberach, Germany (d. 1996)
Louis Chiron, Monégasque racing driver, born in Monte Carlo, Monaco (d. 1979)
Arnold Struycken, Dutch lawyer, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1955)
Ernie Pyle, American journalist and war correspondent during WW II (Pulitzer Prize, 1944), born in Dana, Indiana (d. 1945)
Hugh Dempster, British theatre and film actor (Anna Karenina, Vice Versa, Scrooge), born in London
Bernard Reichel, Swiss composer, born in Neuchâtel, Switzerland (d. 1992)
John Stennis, American politician, Mississippi Senator, born in Kemper County, Mississippi (d. 1995)
Stefan Wyszyński, Polish Cardinal, born in Zuzela, Congress Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1981)
Habib Bourguiba, 1st president of Tunisia (1957-87), born in Monastir, Regency of Tunisia (d. 2000)
Judson Laire, American stage and screen actor (I Remember Mama - "Papa"; Advise and Consent), born in New York City (d. 1979)
Ray Block, French orchestra leader (Ed Sullivan, Jackie Gleason), born in Imperial Territory of Alsace-Lorraine, German Empire (d. 1982)
Regina Jonas, German first woman rabbi, born in Berlin, German Empire (d. 1944)
Clifford D. Simak, American sci-fi author (Hugo, Empire, Way Station), born in Millville, Wisconsin (d. 1988)
Dolores del Rio, Mexican silent screen actress (Deseada, Bugambilia), born in Durango, Mexico (d. 1983)
Maggie Kuhn, American activist and co-founder (Gray Panthers), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1995)
Leonhard Huizinga, Dutch journalist and writer (Adriaan & Olivier), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1980)
Adrienne Ames, American actress (Death Kiss, Panama Patrol), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 1947)
Ernesto Geisel, 29th President of Brazil (1974-79), born in Bento Gonçalves, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil (d. 1996)
Irene Tedrow, American actress (Lucy-Dennis the Menace, Mr Novak), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1995)
Lawrence Brown, American trombonist (Duke Ellington Orchestra), born in Lawrence, Kansas (d. 1988)
Alex McCrindle, Scottish actor (Star Wars, Eye of the Needle), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1990)
Mel Tolkin, Ukrainian television comedy writer, born in Odessa, Ukraine (d. 2007)
Rik Slabbinck, Flemish painter, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 1991)
Sal Santen, Dutch writer (You Are Jewish People), born in Amsterdam (d. 1998)
José Manuel Moreno, Argentine footballer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1978)
Shakeel Badayuni, Indian poet and lyricist, born in Badayun, United Provinces of Agra and Oudh, British India (d. 1970)
(Robert Jordan) "Smoki" Whitfield, American actor (Bomba the Jungle Boy; Lord of the Jungle; Swamp Fox), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Eddie Jefferson, American jazz vocalese singer and lyricist ("Moody's Mood for Love"), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1979)
James MacGregor Burns, American political writer (The Lion & the Fox), born in Melrose, Massachusetts (d. 2014)
Larry Haines [Hecht], American actor (The Odd Couple, Search for Tomorrow), born in Mount Vernon, New York (d. 2008)
Sidney Gottlieb, American CIA official and poison expert, born in The Bronx, New York (d. 1999)
Charlie Shavers, American jazz trumpet player, born in New York City (d. 1971)
Elmar Tampõld, Estonian-Canadian architect, born in Kärdla, Estonia (d. 2013)
Maria Karnilova, American dancer and actress (Fiddler on the Roof, Ivan the Terrible), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2001)
P. D. James [Phyllis Dorothy], Baroness James of Holland Park, English crime writer (Cover Her Face), born in Oxford, England (d. 2014)
Alec Wyton, English-born American composer, born in London, England (d. 2007)
Hayden Carruth, American poet (The Crow and the Heart), born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 2008)
Marilyn Maxwell, American actress and entertainer (Champion, The Lemon Drop Kid, East of Sumatra), born in Clarinda, Iowa (d. 1972)
Richard Adler, American composer and songwriter (The Pajama Game; Damn Yankees), born in New York City (d. 2012)
Robert Sumner, American evangelist and author, born in Norwich, New York (d. 2016)
Anne Klein, American fashion designer (Anne Klein II), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1974)
Roger Foulon, Belgian writer (Un été dans la Fagne), born in Thuin, Belgium (d. 2008)
Shenouda III of Alexandria, Pope of the Coptic Christianity, born in Abnub, Asyut Governorate, Egypt (d. 2012)
(Elizabeth) "Connie" Converse, American folk singer-songwriter, born in Laconia, New Hampshire (disappeared 1974)[1]
James Komack, American writer, director, and actor (The Courtship of Eddie's Father; Chico and the Man; Welcome Back, Kotter), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Leon Uris, American novelist (Exodus; Topaz; QB VII), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2003)
Max van der Stoel, Dutch politician and Minister of Foreign Affairs (PvdA), born in Voorschoten, Netherlands (d. 2011)
Gordon Scott [Werschkul], American actor (Tarzan series), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 2007)
Cécile Aubry, French actress (The Black Rose), author (Belle et Sébastien), television screenwriter and director, born in Paris, France (d. 2010)
Bethel Leslie, American entertainer (Capt Newman MD, Rabbit Trap), born in New York City (d. 1999)
(Averil) Mary Thomas, Welsh lyric soprano of both early and contemporary concert music, born in  Swansea, Wales (d. 1997)
Pat Crawford, Australian cricketer (Australian pace bowler 1956), born in Dubbo, New South Wales, Australia (d. 2009)
